hard.next_step: 2 Jul: buyer asking for module clarifications, solution not clear on business use cases, workshop requested; 25 Jun usage numbers shared for a budgetary proposal; 3 Jul sizing call confirmed iSource + supplier repository scope.
hard.ais_why: Buyer explicitly sought AI software for PR-to-award and wants automated end-to-end railway reporting (strong), but asked zero Merlin/agentic questions and deprioritized Merlin to the AI-assist tier on the 3 Jul call; AI is the means, not the goal.
hard.primary_competitor: Undisclosed vendors in the buyer-run parallel price comparison

north_star_verdict.headline: Live but shaky Qualified sourcing deal: after a 5-19 May discovery/workshop/demo run and a ~4-week gap, Michael Toma pulled a 3 Jul call to re-clarify modules he said were still unclear, and the team narrowed us to a Phase-1 iSource + supplier-repository quote for the railway project. Buyer self-marked confidence at 10% on 2 Jul, is price-comparing us against undisclosed rivals, has engaged no economic buyer, and the owner just changed from Dan Quinn to Mohamad Alhakim on 29 Jun.
north_star_verdict.math: As of 6 Jul 2026 the deal is 55 days old (created 12 May) and 45 days in Qualified (since 22 May) with no forward stage movement. Close date 31 Dec 2026 is 178 days out. Path to close from Qualified through Formal Evaluation, Shortlisted, Vendor Selected, Negotiation and Contract In Progress plus a Zycus-stated 5-8 month implementation cannot credibly finish by 31 Dec on current pace. Deal is engaged (real two-way call 3 Jul, buyer-requested), so it is not cold, but conviction is thinning: scope shrank from an 8-module suite to iSource + repository, buyer confidence self-marked 10% on 2 Jul, and no economic buyer has been met. This is a momentum/conviction problem, not merely a date problem, so it reads Slowing rather than Close Date Risk.
evidence: Deal is engaged today, not dark: a real two-way sizing/scope call ran 3 Jul with Michael Toma and Tarek Ibrahim Yousef, buyer-requested after Michael wrote 1 Jul 'I would like to have some discussion about some of the modules' (Avoma 3 Jul; SF Task 1 Jul).

Scope narrowed defensively from the full S2P suite to iSource plus supplier repository only, iContract explicitly out and iRisk/SRPM performance optional: Tarek said 'At this stage we are just focusing on the supply chain... issue the RFP until we issue the purchase order... Any other module might be discussed later on as an expansion' (Avoma 3 Jul).

No economic buyer engaged. Buyer's own approval chain runs PM to Director to BU Director to Branch to COO and the decision goes to top management, yet only procurement-level contacts have met Zycus (SF Next_Step__c 5 May; OpportunityContactRole: Michael Toma Procurement Team Leader, Tarek Ibrahim Yousef Director Of Procurement).

Buyer is running an active price comparison against unnamed rivals: Tarek said 'we will compare your quotation with other quotations... we will compare technicality and costs, and the conclusion will make our decision in which direction exactly we're gonna proceed' (Avoma 3 Jul).

Confidence self-marked at 10% on 2 Jul with the buyer stating the 'solution not clear with the business use cases' and requesting a workshop, signaling conviction thinning after the 25 Jun proposal share (SF Next_Step__c 2 Jul).

Owner handover mid-cycle: Dan Quinn left Zycus and Mohamad Alhakim (Regional Vice President, Middle East) took the deal 29 Jun, so the buyer relationship is being re-established (SF field history 29 Jun).

Timeline gap: the railway project's contractually-mandated reporting needs procurement running around Aug-Sep 2026, but Zycus's own stated implementation is 5-8 months, so a Phase-1 go-live realistically lands Q1 2027, a gap the buyer has not acknowledged (SF Next_Step__c 5 May; Zycus implementation range stated on calls).

metrics.narrative: The pain is well articulated but not yet quantified. On the 5 May discovery Orascom described four operational failures: 100% of RFQs and bids run over email/Excel with no audit trail or digital evaluation; manual procurement reporting is failing a client contractual requirement on a major railway project (legal/contractual exposure); a multi-level approval chain (PM to Director to BU Director to Branch to COO) is unmanaged; and there is no status visibility across international logistics milestones. No cost, savings, cycle-time, or penalty figure has been attached to any of these, so quantifying the railway-compliance exposure and the manual-effort cost is the open move (SF Next_Step__c 5 May SITUATION/IMPACT).

economic_buyer.narrative: The economic buyer is unmapped and unengaged. Tarek Ibrahim Yousef (Director Of Procurement) is the most senior person Zycus has met and directs the evaluation, but on 3 Jul he framed the decision as one that goes upward: 'we will compare your quotation with other quotations... the conclusion will make our decision,' and earlier notes describe presenting to top management. The buyer's own stated approval chain (PM to Director to BU Director to Branch to COO) puts real budget authority at BU-Director/Branch/COO level, none of whom has appeared on any call. The move is to reach the railway-project budget owner through Tarek before the price comparison concludes (Avoma 3 Jul; SF Next_Step__c 5 May).

decision_criteria.narrative: The criteria are explicit and buyer-stated. Orascom is evaluating a tightly-scoped iSource capability on both technicality and cost against parallel vendor quotations: RFQ creation through bid analysis, supplier awarding and PO issuance, a mandatory supplier repository imported from Oracle, and integration back to Oracle Fusion to feed finance and stores. Tarek was emphatic that the comparison be like-for-like: 'we want to have this comparison on the same basis... if you will quote 150 users your price will hike up and do not be into the competition,' which is why he pinned scope to 30 users (25 business, 5 power). Contract management is deliberately excluded from the scored scope (Avoma 3 Jul).
sources: SF Email 25 Jun 2026 (30 users / 63 months request)

decision_process.narrative: The process is a procurement-led competitive quotation feeding an internal approval. Orascom is collecting like-for-like quotes from Zycus and other vendors, comparing technicality and cost, then taking a recommendation to top management through the PM-to-COO approval chain. The call sequence itself shows the buyer still in scope-definition, not selection: the 3 Jul session existed to remove 'misunderstanding and confusing from the bill of material' and confirm modules. No decision date has been named, and no evaluator above procurement has joined, so the process is real but early and single-layered (Avoma 3 Jul; SF Next_Step__c 5 May).

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics are not yet in view and are correctly deferred at this stage. Orascom explicitly pushed contract management out of Phase-1 scope ('Contract will not be in scope... that will be handled outside separately'), and no MSA, Order Form, SOW, legal, procurement-portal, or InfoSec discussion has occurred. As a new-business sourcing deal this would eventually need an Order Form plus a SOW (SOW signed separately by the AVP Global Delivery) rather than a full-suite MSA weighting. Visibility will come only once the buyer selects a vendor and shares its contracting and legal channel (Avoma 3 Jul).

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is concrete and owner-attributable. Orascom's custom procurement system (Compass) was retired, leaving a sourcing gap that Oracle Fusion does not fill: it handles PRs, warehouse and finance but nothing between PR and PO, so RFQ creation, bid management, evaluation and awarding all run manually over email/Excel. The forcing pain is a new railway infrastructure project whose client contract mandates automated end-to-end procurement reporting, putting Orascom at contractual risk today. The pain owners are the procurement team (Michael Toma, Tarek Ibrahim Yousef) and, above them, the railway project delivery leadership (SF Next_Step__c 5 May; Avoma 3 Jul).

champion.narrative: There is no true champion. Michael Toma (Procurement Team Leader, Decision Maker in SF) is the most active contact and pulled the 3 Jul call, but his engagement is reactive and confused rather than advocative, he asked for the module walkthrough because 'what he agreed with Dan is different than what he expected.' Tarek Ibrahim Yousef (Director Of Procurement) directs the evaluation and controls the scope discipline but is a neutral evaluator, not selling Zycus internally. Neither has introduced an executive, driven urgency around the railway deadline, or defended Zycus against the parallel quotes, so building a champion with railway-project accountability is a top continuous move (Avoma 3 Jul; OpportunityContactRole).

competition.narrative: Three fronts. Oracle is the incumbent ERP (Oracle Fusion) whose sourcing capability Orascom found insufficient between PR and PO, giving Zycus a workflow-depth wedge, but Oracle stays as the integration backbone and could re-offer its sourcing module. Unnamed vendors are being quoted in parallel: Tarek confirmed 'we will compare your quotation with other quotations,' so a live like-for-like bake-off is underway even though no rival is named. The third and arguably strongest current threat is no-decision, the buyer staying on email/Excel or delaying, given confidence dropped to 10% and scope is shrinking. Zycus wins by proving sourcing depth plus clean Oracle Fusion integration and construction-sector fit, and by tying the railway contractual deadline to a fast Phase-1 go-live (Avoma 3 Jul; SF Next_Step__c 2 Jul).

deal_movement.summary: After a dense 5-19 May discovery/workshop/demo/estimation-tables run, the deal went quiet for ~4 weeks, amount doubled to $200k on 15 Jun, the buyer asked for a 30-user budgetary offer on 25 Jun, ownership moved from Dan Quinn to Mohamad Alhakim on 29 Jun, and a buyer-requested 3 Jul sizing call narrowed scope to iSource + supplier repository. Confidence was self-marked at 10% on 2 Jul citing unclear business-use-case fit.

competitive_position.summary: The single strongest current threat is a stalled/no-decision outcome: confidence self-marked 10% on 2 Jul, scope shrinking to Phase-1, and the buyer still comparing quotes with no executive engaged. Undisclosed vendors are in a live parallel price comparison (Tarek, 3 Jul) and rank as the active competitive front even though unnamed. Oracle is the incumbent ERP (Oracle Fusion) whose PR-to-PO sourcing gap opened the door and which must be integrated with, not displaced. Zycus's wedge is sourcing workflow depth plus clean Oracle integration and construction fit, but it has not been reinforced since the 19 May demo and the 2 Jul confusion suggests it did not fully land.

best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy levers matter now: multi-thread above procurement to the railway budget owner, convert technical fit into a quantified compliance business case, and re-anchor the relationship fast after the owner handover before the 10% confidence hardens.
flags: Multi-thread beyond procurement to the railway project director and the BU-Director/COO budget owner before the parallel quotes are benchmarked.
Turn the strong-but-unquantified pain into a railway-compliance ROI case (contractual-risk and manual-effort cost) to beat do-nothing and justify price versus the undisclosed rivals.
Re-establish the relationship quickly after the Dan Quinn to Mohamad Alhakim handover so the 2 Jul 10%-confidence slide does not calcify into a stall.

win_position.text: We are a credible technical fit: Orascom retired their Compass sourcing system and Oracle Fusion cannot cover RFQ-to-award, so Michael Toma pulled a 3 Jul call to scope an iSource quote (Avoma, 3 Jul).
Scope shrank defensively from an 8-module S2P suite to iSource plus supplier repository, with iContract cut and iRisk optional, framed by Tarek as Phase-1 for a clean price comparison (Avoma, 3 Jul).
No economic buyer is engaged: Tarek said the decision goes to top management through a PM-to-COO chain and only procurement-level contacts have met us (Avoma, 3 Jul; SF Next_Step, 5 May).
Orascom is actively price-comparing us against undisclosed vendors: Tarek, 'we will compare your quotation with other quotations... the conclusion will make our decision' (Avoma, 3 Jul).

deal_momentum.text: Momentum is choppy: a 5-19 May discovery/workshop/demo run was followed by roughly four weeks of quiet, then Michael requested the 3 Jul call to re-clarify modules he said were still unclear (SF Next_Step, 2 Jul; Avoma, 3 Jul).
Ownership changed mid-cycle: Dan Quinn left Zycus and Mohamad Alhakim (RVP Middle East) took the deal on 29 Jun, so the buyer relationship is being re-established (SF field history, 29 Jun).
Commercials are live: the buyer requested a budgetary offer for 30 users over 63 months on 25 Jun and confirmed 25 business plus 5 power users on 3 Jul (SF Email, 25 Jun; Avoma, 3 Jul).

customer_commitment.text: The buyer is sizing a real quote and pulled the 3 Jul session themselves, defining users and locking Phase-1 scope for a like-for-like comparison (Avoma, 3 Jul).
Commitment is capped at the procurement layer: the buyer is still assembling a cost-and-technical comparison to take upward, with no budget owner met and no decision date named (Avoma, 3 Jul).
Confidence was self-marked at 10% on 2 Jul because the solution was 'not clear with the business use cases,' prompting the workshop request (SF Next_Step, 2 Jul).

deal_risk.text: Timeline gap: the railway project's mandated reporting needs procurement live around Aug-Sep 2026, but Zycus's own stated implementation is 5-8 months, so Phase-1 cannot go live before Q1 2027 (SF Next_Step, 5 May; Avoma implementation range).

No-decision risk dominates: with scope shrinking, confidence at 10%, and module confusion, the biggest threat is Orascom staying on email/Excel or delaying rather than losing to a named rival (SF Next_Step, 2 Jul; Avoma, 3 Jul).
